title: Conceptual constraints and diagnostics

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Conceptual constraints and diagnostics; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Concepts and requires expressions state compile-time semantic and syntactic requirements, improving overload participation and diagnostics without testing runtime values.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A sorting helper requires random-access iteration and sortable values, rejecting a list iterator at the call boundary. Your goal is to explain why an instantiation fails and improve its requirement or call site. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Standard algorithms; Class interface and invariants; Function templates; Warnings and diagnostics. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Concepts and requires expressions state compile-time semantic and syntactic requirements, improving overload participation and diagnostics without testing runtime values. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: A syntactically valid expression does not prove every semantic law, overly broad concepts accept broken behavior, and constraints do not replace runtime preconditions. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: A requires expression executes sample operations at runtime and proves the algorithm will succeed for every value.

includes: State template requirements and read substitution diagnostics from the first failed requirement.
Explain why an instantiation fails and improve its requirement or call site.
excludes: A syntactically valid expression does not prove every semantic law, overly broad concepts accept broken behavior, and constraints do not replace runtime preconditions.

outcomes: Explain why an instantiation fails and improve its requirement or call site.

examples.title: Conceptual constraints and diagnostics: defined case
examples.situation: A sorting helper requires random-access iteration and sortable values, rejecting a list iterator at the call boundary.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A sorting helper requires random-access iteration and sortable values, rejecting a list iterator at the call boundary.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Constraints are compile-time requirements on types and expressions; document semantic laws and validate runtime value preconditions separately.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Constraints are compile-time requirements on types and expressions; document semantic laws and validate runtime value preconditions separately.

examples.title: Conceptual constraints and diagnostics: boundary case
examples.situation: A numeric concept checks available operations but documentation still states nonzero divisor as a runtime precondition.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A numeric concept checks available operations but documentation still states nonzero divisor as a runtime precondition.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: A syntactically valid expression does not prove every semantic law, overly broad concepts accept broken behavior, and constraints do not replace runtime preconditions.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: A requires expression executes sample operations at runtime and proves the algorithm will succeed for every value.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Explain why an instantiation fails and improve its requirement or call site.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A requires expression executes sample operations at runtime and proves the algorithm will succeed for every value.
misconceptions.correction: Constraints are compile-time requirements on types and expressions; document semantic laws and validate runtime value preconditions separately.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: A requires expression executes sample operations at runtime and proves the algorithm will succeed for every value.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Conceptual constraints and diagnostics: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
A syntactically valid expression does not prove every semantic law, overly broad concepts accept broken behavior, and constraints do not replace runtime preconditions.

recap.summary: You should now be able to explain why an instantiation fails and improve its requirement or call site. Keep this boundary in view: A syntactically valid expression does not prove every semantic law, overly broad concepts accept broken behavior, and constraints do not replace runtime preconditions.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Conceptual constraints and diagnostics using a concrete CPP rule rather than the optional analogy.
Why is this claim incorrect: A requires expression executes sample operations at runtime and proves the algorithm will succeed for every value.
Apply the checkpoint 'compile-time requirements' to the boundary case from memory.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “variant and type-safe alternatives”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
